sentences.text: In-- it's a long story.
In 1937 or '38, I had a correspondence with a young girl in America, in Newark, New Jersey, who-- I had studied English in high school, and she studied German in high school.
We-- we had a-- a correspondence.
And at one point, my father met, in Dresden, a man who-- who married that-- that's how I got to the girl.
My father met a man in Newark, New Jersey, who had-- in Dresden, who had a sister in Newark, New Jersey.
And my father had an uncle around the turn of the century, to-- and when he was in America for two years-- my father, as a child-- and he went back.
To make a long story short, we had a correspondence with that-- with that girl.
And at the end of the war-- when we were in Rome, we tried to take up contact with them.
You-- you try to establish roots again-- connections with people.
And one of the letters reached her.
And she started, right way, to-- to send us some money and to send us some packages of what she think we need.
And she went to my father's family, asked him, you have to help these people to come to America.
And-- and they turned her down.
And she told the story to-- to a-- I mentioned-- to a family she knew.
And he said, well, I know the Dressel family.
They are quite comfortable.
And I'm going to talk to them.
And he went to them, and they refused to-- he refused to help them.
So this man got angry and-- and said, well, you are the Dressel's relative.
I was going to give you the privilege of bringing them to this country.
But if you can't do it, I'm going to it.
And with this, he-- he-- at that time, you had to have an affidavit of support.
You had to have somebody vouching for you that you will not become a public charge, just like today.
What was this young girl's name, who played such a strong role--
Her name was--
--at that time, was Edna Umschweis-- U-M-S-C-H-W-E-I-S.
She's now Edna Fried-- F-R-I-E-D.
And we are still friends.
It was the Schreiber family-- a family-- S-C-H-R-E-I-B-E-R-- who were well-established people in Newark, New Jersey.
Yeah.
The-- their son is.
We are in contact with the son and-- and-- and their daughter.
They live in Florida.
And-- and we-- we see them.
We're still friendly with them.
Well, she--
In 1957-- in 1956, I think, December, 1956, she moved-- she-- her husband was in business.
She moved to an apartment in Irving, New Jersey.
I went to help her move, and she said, you know?
I-- I have a friend.
I'm going to give you her address, if you want to call her.
It was a blind date.
And I waited until January the next year.
And I finally called that young girl.
And it turned out it was Martha.
And we got married October, that year.
And that was-- was the best thing that ever happened to me.
And-- and, I think, to Martha, too.
And we were both very blessed with a very happy marriage.
[SIGHS] Well, the people who sponsored us brought us over there, prepared an apartment for us.
And-- and between that Edna and-- and the Schreiber family, they helped us.
After two weeks, the Schreiber son said, Henry, it's time for you to go to work.
And he gave me a job in his business.
He had a-- a distributor of-- a wholesale distributor of photographic supplies.
And I worked there for many years.
It was a family business.
And my sister became an operator, got work very soon.
And it was a slow beginning, but we were very happy.
And we were-- it was interesting.
We lived in a new country, and we were free.
And it-- it was hard.
Any-- any new start is hard.
But I worked in that company, until 1959, I think, for many years.
And I got married in 1957.
And Martha had a-- has a very nice family.
And she had three sisters at that time-- one-- two.
One isn't here anymore.
And her mother.
And a very good family, a very close family too.
And I was used to a close family.
And my sister died young, in 1954.
She was married a few years.
And she left a little boy behind.
My mother died in 1956.
And we got married in 1957.
And my father got remarried again, later.
And he got-- he died in 1971.
In Newark, New Jersey.
Well, when we got married--
No, we lived in Newark, New Jersey.
Yeah.
No.
Yes, but not-- not in the detail, for instance, that I just talked here.
We talked about it.
I never made a secret out of it.
If people asked, I would tell them.
But I always had a feeling that people either were embarrassed to ask, or they didn't want to ask, because they thought it would hurt me when I talk about it.
I guess there was a need for me to talk about it.
And when the picture came out, that-- in 1980-- no, when the book came out in 1982, that started changing my life a little bit.
And I was very much tempted to-- to write to Thomas Keneally, the author of the book, to be-- to give him my-- my input.
But then, I was afraid-- I didn't talk about it at all.
And I was afraid, again, what-- what would it do to my life?
So I-- I-- I let it go.
And when the book was published, I bought a few copies of it.
And I sent to to all-- to-- to friends of ours, [INAUDIBLE] to-- to Martha's family, and so on.
And I-- I put a little inscription in it.
And I may-- I'll read this for you.
Here's what I wrote.

It was in 1982. 'Dear friend, this true story of Oskar Schindler's author in frightening detail the story of my-- my parents and my sister's life between 1939 and May, 1945 in Krakow, the ghetto in Krakow, Podgorze, the concentration camps in Krakow, Plaszow, Gross-Rosen, and Auschwitz, and Schindler's labor camp in Brinnlitz, Czechoslovakia, from which we were liberated by the Russians on May 9, 1945.
I knew most characters in the book-- Germans and Jews-- personally.
I knew every street and place.
We lived there.
We were in every happening and action related in the book.
Reading this book brought back many painful memories of names and events long forgotten.
And it made me realize the absolute miracle of our reunification and survival in Brinnlitz.
I want the people close to me to have this book.
And I urge you and, if possible, your children to read it for three reasons.
Number one, a selfish one, for me.
To give you an almost intimate glimpse into our everyday life during an era of which very little is known, except statistics, and during which life was not always the better choice.
Life was not always the better choice.

Two, to make you better appreciate your own life with whatever trials and tribulations it may hold and to realize how fortunate you and your children were to be spared all this by the geographic accident of your living in this country.
Three, to impress upon you that there are still many good people in this world of evil.

I know, for it is only because of a handful of good people-- Jews, Germans, and other non-Jews alike-- that my family and I survived as a family unit, that I am able today to write this and to give you this book in appreciation of my life, of which you are now a part.' And it was signed Henry.
And written in 1982, the inscription, in the books that I sent to my family and so on.
Well, again, the same thing came up.
I heard, it was in the papers that said, Spielberg would-- would make that picture.
And again, I was tempted to-- to offer him my input, but I didn't do it.
Then, when the picture was published, it created an incredible interest, an incredible awareness.
And we were sitting around the pool in Florida, and some people started asking questions.
They knew I was a-- a survivor.
And they said-- the questions were basically the same, most of the time.
And I said, well, maybe I ought to talk to them and make a talk out of it.
I spoke to our social committee, and they said, all right.
Make it set in the evening.
And we invited them.
I expected 30 or 40 people, but 150 showed up.
We had to borrow chairs from the Town Hall and-- and-- and coffee urns from-- from-- from a neighborhood committee.
And I spoke.
And the mayor of our town and a couple of commissioners of our town were there.
And without my knowledge, they informed the local newspaper.
And it sort of started the ball rolling.
And the reception was incredible.
And then, one led to the other.
I've since spoken at about, at least, 25 times in different places, in schools, and colleges, organizations.
I spoke at the County Bar Association.
I have spoken in-- in the Victim's Rights Coalition, at affairs.
And I was invited to St.
Louis a couple of months ago, to be the guest speaker at a dedication for a learning-- a Holocaust Museum and learning Center.
And I've been-- I've been quite busy with these things.
I never thought I would do it.
I never thought I could do.
But somehow, things I knew that you-- that you can do, that you don't know.
Well, I think Spielberg did a tremendous job.
People ask me, is it-- how accurate?
It is very accurate.
I think it's understated, as far as the atrocities are concerned.
And some things are-- are dramatized.
That's all right.
That's all right with me that he understated the atrocities, too.
I think he did a very good job.
He deserved whatever awards he got for it.

But I think his greatest accomplishment is that he created the interest for the Holocaust again, because all these things that happened in the last year-and-a-half with-- with the-- with the-- with the awareness and the discussion of-- of-- of-- of the Holocaust.

And then, in connection with what's going on in the world today, in Somalia, and Yugoslavia, and Luanda, and all these places, where there are wars, and there are people killed just because of hatred, or intolerance, or-- or just ignorance, or-- that it is so important that these things be preserved in, like the tape, what I speak today, that these things will be preserved and brought to the people's knowledge, that they see what can happen, if these things are not controlled.
Whether it will succeed, I don't know.
Maybe it's a utopia, that you could never have peace.
But-- but you have to try.
And I feel very strongly about it, that people should be told that there were good people who helped, who-- who did-- who were not bystanders.
There were millions of people who-- who didn't actively take part, but who were bystanders-- let-- let it happen.
There were a few people who-- like Schindler, or like [INAUDIBLE], or the-- the-- the-- the Polish woman that smuggled some milk into the camp-- they-- they became rescuers to us.
And I think it's important.
They were not born heroes.
These were just plain, ordinary people, but who found they-- they had that sense that something has to be done when they saw something wrong.
And I think that has to be-- that thought has to be told to the world.
When I met Mrs.
Schindler, and she was in Palm Beach, in Florida, in November, we met her at a reception.
And it was quite an-- I saw her.
After 50 years, she's a woman of 87 years.
And she said to me a couple of things.
And one of these things she said-- first of all, when I took her picture, I tell her-- we asked her to take a picture-- she said, don't come too close.
That shows the wrinkles too much.
[LAUGHS] But then she said, don't put me on a pedestal.
It-- it's lonely up there.
But then she said, when you see somebody on the street who-- who's lying down on the street, and he cannot get up by himself, every decent man will try to help him get up.
And we-- I would do the same thing again, today.
And I think that was-- she expressed this very, very nicely.
Not really.
I tried to resume contact with [NON-ENGLISH], but that hasn't worked too well.
He's in Germany.
I don't know if he's still in business.
I have spoken to-- to Leopold Pfefferberg [INAUDIBLE] a few times on the telephone.
Otherwise, I still do not want the Holocaust to be the-- the main event in my life.
The main element in my life is to live a-- a happy and peaceful life, as much as possible.
This is a-- the last year and a half have been very exciting for me, but I'm glad that I didn't speak 50 years ago, because it would have changed my-- the course of my life.
I would have been too involved in it.
Now, you have a little more distance, and I can handle it.
Yes.
Once, yes.
It-- I don't know.
It was-- must have been in the '60s.
As I recall, from Germany, they asked me to testify about one of the Germans in-- in the-- in the ghetto.
And I-- they sent over somebody-- an attorney, I guess-- to give a deposition about it, which I did.
And-- and we lived in Elizabeth at that time.
And that was about it.
I was never asked, and I wouldn't have gone to Germany to testify.
And so that's-- that's about the story.
Of course, now, I've-- I haven't forgotten a lot.
But now, during the last year, I'm reading some more.
And I'm more interested again.
And I'm cutting out newspaper things that have any relevance to-- to what I went through.
And I'm-- have a scrapbook.
And I have, from the episode of my talking has been very-- the episodes of my talking has been very, very satisfying.
I have talked to school children, who are very, very interested.
I've talked to high school students.
And these, to me, are clarifying experiences.
[INAUDIBLE] ?
No.
No, I have not.
We were on a tourist trip once in-- it was-- it was a group, a traveling group.
We were in Vienna, and--
Must have been in the '80s, early '80s, or late '70s.
And I was very much tempted to go into Dresden, but it was so complicated.
It was through the Eastern zone.
And you needed visas.
And it would've taken six months to get all the permits, and then, for-- for a few hours in Dresden.
And Martha was very happy that I didn't go.
And I got it out of my system.
Oh, yes.
Yes, I do.
I have them, not as much as I used to.
But then, for a few days in a row, I will have-- I'll have dreams about this, yes.
And you know, you can't just shape-- shake this off, you know?
It hangs onto you.
It's-- for me-- usually, they count the Holocaust the years of 1939 to 1945.
But for me, it started in 1933, for people who lived in Germany.
So that's 12 years of your-- of your young life.
And that's a long time.
And you don't-- and-- and the-- the time after the Holocaust-- after the Holocaust, which we discussed, in-- in 10 minutes, so many things happened.
We went through so many things, too.
It's-- it's-- it's half a lifetime.
Well, I think our priorities are a little different.
I have changed a lot since then.

When we came to this country, and we went to visit some people, and they-- they wanted to put a new mirror up over the sofa, and they didn't know should they put an oval or a rectangular mirror on it.
I said, gee, this is ridiculous.
Why do they-- people worry about?
But now, as you live in a-- in-- among-- amidst civilization, you adjust to this.
And I'm just as much in these things now as-- as other people are.
And I'm grateful for my life.
I'm-- I appreciate what people have done for us.
And sometimes feel that I owe, not to any individual, but I owe to society.
And I like to lead a good and decent life.
Oh.
Give me-- give me a hint.
[LAUGHTER]
I-- I don't know.
Well, we always had a good, close family relationship.
And I think that helped through all the war years.
I think that the people who lost their families, were on their own, had nobody to consider, they would take more chances.
And we had a good life.
And I have a good life now.
And I hope it keeps up like this.
And I-- I wish I would be free, peace.
I'm very nervous about what's going on in the country-- the-- the mood of confrontation, confrontation, the mood of-- of hatred.
Instead of-- instead of cooperating, they-- they-- they confront each other and try-- everybody wants to have his-- his way is the only way.
And the world cannot live like this.
And I'm-- I'm concerned about this.
And one of the reasons I have, maybe, some of these things when-- that I brought to the attention of general public and general-- in different countries, maybe it will rub a little bit off.
Maybe they say, gee, you don't have to follow.
The school kids said, if we can tell the school, the students, maybe, you don't have to-- there's a leader who-- who leads you on to some things.
Maybe you don't have to do it, if you don't feel like.
Maybe you can just think about it.
And this is what we really have to do.
The-- the Holocaust is no longer a-- a Jewish-- it is part of Jewish history, but it-- it's not only Jewish history.
It is part of the history of World War II.
It's part of world history-- all countries were involved, through action, or through non-action.
It is something that concerns the world, and not only Jews.
And that has to be brought to the attention of people.
And I hope that, whatever Spielberg is doing with this, and I hope whatever I'm doing, or the different museums, that that has a little influence on the world.
If it does, I don't know.
But you have to try.
I'll be happy to.
I'd like you to meet Martha, my wife, Martha Dressler.
We have been married since 1957.
Is that right?
Mm-hm.
That's correct.
Yes, I would.
When anybody asks me, how come Henry survived?
I have this one thing to say.
I said, he had to survive to come to America to find me.
I think it's a wonderful idea.
It's going to be in all the museums.
And it's part of history.
And it's an important factor.
Well, I-- I would say that one person can make a difference.
And I also want to add the fact that all the things that Henry went through, he is still a wonderful person and a good man.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
You too.
